issues.title: The public library API and the <very-nice-mermaid> custom element never call the detectType router — any non-flowchart DSL fed directly (not via the CLI) is silently misparsed into a garbage flowchart
issues.description: mount() (src/render/dom/index.ts) calls prepare() (src/render/prepare.ts) for any plain string input, and prepare() calls parse() (the v1 flowchart-only parser) unconditionally — it never calls classify() (src/mermaid/router.ts). The same is true of the other top-level convenience functions: renderSvg() (src/render/svg.ts) and renderHtml()/renderAscii()/renderMarkdown() only special-case an ALREADY-CONSTRUCTED SequenceLayout/ClassLayout/StateLayout object; for a raw DSL string they fall through to the same prepare()->parse() path. The <very-nice-mermaid> custom element (src/element.ts) calls mount(this, dsl, {theme}) directly with the raw inline text content, so it inherits the same gap. Only src/cli/run.ts actually orchestrates classify() -> dispatch -> native read+layout (doSequenceRender/doClassRender/doStateRender) or renderFallbackSvg(); every e2e class/sequence/state spec drives HTML that was PRE-COMPUTED by the CLI (exportHtml() shells out to `vnm render`) and only mounts the already-positioned model client-side, so none of them exercise mount()/the custom element with a raw non-flowchart DSL string. Verified hands-on: built minimal pages embedding dist/element.js with <very-nice-mermaid> containing (a) a sequenceDiagram, (b) an erDiagram, (c) a stateDiagram-v2, (d) a gantt chart as inline text. All four render as WRONG, misleading output with zero diagnostic: the sequence/ER/gantt DSLs each produce one disconnected flowchart card per keyword/line (e.g. 'sequenceDiagram','participant','User','API','DB' as five unrelated boxes; 'erDiagram','CUSTOMER','ORDER','string','int','date' as six unrelated boxes) with NO edges at all; the stateDiagram-v2 is worse because it partially 'succeeds' — the old flowchart parser's `-->` grammar coincidentally captures some transitions, but drops every transition label (pause/resume/stop/fault all vanish), drops the [*] start/end pseudo-states entirely, and fabricates a fake orphan 'stateDiagram' node from the header keyword — a plausible-looking but subtly wrong diagram, which is more dangerous than an obviously-broken one. This is the exact 'silent misparse' bug FR1 explicitly promised to fix ('Unknown/invalid -> a clear diagnostic ... never a garbage flowchart') and directly contradicts FR6 ('Browser-first rendering: the library + HTML export render every tier in the browser') — it is fixed ONLY on the CLI's internal code path, not on the library's actual public/browser-facing surface (mount, renderSvg, renderHtml, renderAscii/renderMarkdown, and the <very-nice-mermaid> element, which is one of only two package.json `exports` entries).
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE. Wire classify(dsl) into the shared entry points that currently call prepare()/parse() directly on a raw string: in prepare() (or a new shared `resolveInput()` used by mount/renderSvg/renderHtml/renderAscii/renderMarkdown), when input is a string, call classify() first (mirroring src/cli/run.ts's flow): native non-flowchart tiers (sequence/class/state) -> call the matching readXModel()+layoutX() and return that PositionedModel/Layout; fallback tier -> call renderFallbackSvg() (browser DOM in-browser, jsdom in Node) and wrap for the pan/zoom shell; unclassifiable/garbage -> surface the same clear diagnostic the CLI emits instead of silently parsing as flowchart. renderSvg/renderHtml/renderAscii already special-case pre-built SequenceLayout/ClassLayout/StateLayout objects, so the fix is to also do the classify+dispatch step when given a raw string, not just when given an already-typed layout. Add e2e coverage: mount a non-flowchart DSL (e.g. a stateDiagram-v2 and a pie) directly via the custom element with inline text (no CLI pre-render) and assert it renders the correct diagram type, not flowchart-shaped garbage.
issues.fix_summary: Routed raw DSL through detectType across the library surface. Sync renderSvg/renderHtml/renderAscii/renderMarkdown now throw a clear 'use the async API' error for an explicit non-flowchart string (ensureSyncRenderable + explicitNonFlowchartType) instead of misparsing it. New async twins renderSvgAsync/renderHtmlAsync/renderAsciiAsync/renderMarkdownAsync (src/render/route.ts) run detectType and dispatch exactly like the CLI: flowchart(sync own-parser), sequence/class/state(native re-skin), everything else(mermaid.js fallback). mount() returns its handle synchronously and finishes a non-flowchart render async via a DeferredHandle; mountAsync() resolves the settled handle; the fallback tier reuses the seqRuntime pan/zoom shell; the <very-nice-mermaid> element awaits mountAsync. Browser-safe core preserved (mermaid/jsdom stay dynamic-import-only; dist/index.js + dist/element.js carry no new static Node/external import). ADDED test/route.test.ts driving mount(), mountAsync() AND the element with raw sequence/class/state/pie — native types render native shapes (no flowchart .vnm-node garbage / no fabricated header nodes), fallback pie renders the mermaid SVG. README documents the sync-vs-async contract. ROUND 2 RE-TEST (2026-07-04, the money check) — done in a REAL bundled browser (not Node/jsdom): built a Vite harness (scratch dir, npm-packed dist/ tarball as the dependency, so mermaid resolves through a real bundler), served over http://localhost (not file://), imported mountAsync from the built 'very-nice-mermaid' package and drove it with the gogo-playwright MCP. Raw sequence/class/state DSL strings each rendered their NATIVE shape (sequence: 0 .vnm-node, lifelines + messages, no 'sequenceDiagram' header node; class: 5 .vnm-node compartment cards incl. Animal/Dog/Collar/Vet/Vaccine with fields+methods, no 'classDiagram' header node; state: state cards with start/pause/resume/stop transitions, no 'stateDiagram' header node); a raw pie DSL rendered via the mermaid fallback (real mermaid <svg> with aria-roledescription="pie", legend/percentages, 0 .vnm-node). Also drove the raw <very-nice-mermaid> custom element directly (not just mountAsync) with a hostile class DSL containing an inline <script> in a member label: rendered inert (0 <script> tags in the DOM, a page-level flag the payload tries to set stayed false, 0 console errors). Zero console errors across every case. Screenshots: test/screenshots/round2-real-browser-lib-routing-all.png (all four mounted side by side), round2-real-browser-{sequence,class,state,pie}-native.png. CONFIRMED FIXED — holds in a genuine bundled-browser load, not just the Node/jsdom unit tests.

issues.title: Class native SVG: composition diamond marker renders at a shared edge-routing trunk, visually implying sibling association/dependency relations are also compositions
issues.description: Rendering fixtures/shop-class.mmd (Dog *-- Collar : has [composition]; Dog --> Vet : visits [association]; Dog ..> Vaccine : uses [dependency]) to SVG/HTML at any theme: the three edges leaving Dog toward Collar/Vet/Vaccine share a single routing trunk before fanning out (same edge-bundling behavior as flowchart's multi-branch edges). The composition's filled diamond start-marker is drawn at that SHARED trunk point, so it visually appears to belong to all three relations, not just 'has'. Confirmed via zoomed screenshot (class-light-static-svg-zoom.png, class-dark-static-svg.png): a single filled diamond sits directly below the Dog card, with has/visits/uses all radiating from beneath it. 'visits' (plain association, should have no diamond) and 'uses' (dependency, dashed, should have no diamond) both visually read as compositions. This undercuts the plan's flagship class-diagram feature (typed UML relation markers) and is worse than plain mermaid.js output in this specific case, since mermaid does not bundle relation paths from the same source node this way.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE. When multiple outgoing relations share a routing trunk from the same node, do not draw a relation-specific start marker (diamond/triangle) at the shared segment — only where the edge's own individual path begins (after the fork point), or give edges carrying a distinct start marker (composition/aggregation/inheritance) a small dedicated stub near the source node before joining any shared trunk, so the marker is unambiguously attached to one edge. Add a regression test rendering a node with 3 outgoing relations of different types (one composition, two non-diamond) and asserting exactly one diamond element exists, positioned on the composition edge's own path.
issues.fix_summary: Fixed in the shared geometry via a new computePortOffsets() (src/geometry/index.ts): edges sharing a (node, border-side) are spread onto distinct parallel channels, so a relation's start marker sits on its own edge's start point instead of the shared trunk. sidePoint() applies a clamped perpendicular offset; offsets ride on RoutedEdge.ports so applyPositions()/state shrink reproduce them. Regression test (test/class-svg.test.ts) asserts exactly one composition diamond (marker-start diamond-solid) and that no sibling edge shares its start point. ROUND 2 RE-TEST (2026-07-04): CLI-rendered fixtures/shop-class.mmd to SVG (light theme) and inspected the raw markup directly: the 'has'/'visits'/'uses' edges leaving Dog now start at three DISTINCT x offsets on Dog's bottom border (207.5, 227.5, 247.5 — 20px apart), and marker-start="url(#vnm-cls-diamond-solid)" appears ONLY on the 'has' path; 'visits' and 'uses' carry the plain open-arrow marker at their own end, not the diamond. Zoomed screenshot test/screenshots/round2-class-static-svg-fan.png confirms visually — the filled diamond sits on has's own stub, not a shared trunk. CONFIRMED FIXED in the static SVG/PNG renderer (the only surface that draws relation-type markers — interactive DOM never draws them, a pre-existing, unrelated tradeoff).

issues.title: State native SVG: anti-parallel transitions between the same two states fully occlude one another — one transition (and its label) becomes completely invisible, not merely overlapping
issues.description: Rendering fixtures/order-state.mmd (Running --> Paused : pause; Paused --> Running : resume) to SVG/HTML at any theme: both transitions route on the identical path between Running and Paused. Confirmed via zoomed screenshot (state-light-zoom-antiparallel.png) and an accessibility-tree dump showing both 'pause' and 'resume' text labels stacked within 7px of each other at effectively the same coordinates: only ONE arrow/label ('resume', pointing up into Running) is visible in the rendered image; 'pause' (Running -> Paused) is completely hidden underneath it, including its arrowhead and label. This is more severe than 'overlapping edges' (the review's phrasing) or a routing aesthetic nit — a user viewing this diagram cannot tell that Running -> Paused exists at all; it visually reads as if Paused can only transition back to Running, never the reverse. Data is correct (confirmed: both transitions ARE in the model/DOM), but the render loses real information to the eye. REOPENED round 2 (2026-07-04): the round-5 fix (computePortOffsets in src/geometry + src/layout) genuinely fixes this for the STATIC SVG/PNG renderer (confirmed: fixtures/order-state.mmd -> SVG now shows Running<->Paused and Idle<->Running each on two distinct 20px-apart channels, both arrowheads visible — see test/screenshots/round2-state-static-svg-antiparallel.png). But the fix was never ported into `src/render/dom/runtime.ts` — the self-contained, `.toString()`-serialized edge router that `mount()`, `mountAsync()`, the `<very-nice-mermaid>` element, AND the CLI's own `-f html` export all actually use for the LIVE/interactive rendering. Its `routeEdgePath()`/`anchor()` functions never read `edge.ports` at all (grep-confirmed: no reference to `.ports` anywhere in runtime.ts), so they recompute a single anchor point per (node, side) with zero offset — reproducing the EXACT original bug in the primary interactive surface. Proof: opened the CLI's own canonical `vnm render fixtures/order-state.mmd -f html` output in a real browser (test/screenshots/round2-state-interactive-html-antiparallel-occluded.png) — 'start' (Idle->Running) and 'stop' (Running->Idle) render at literally the same point (both label rects at x=551/552, y=481, 1px apart) and only 'stop' is visible; 'resume' (Paused->Running) is visible but 'pause' (Running->Paused) is fully hidden underneath it (raw DOM dump: the 'pause' and 'resume' `<path>` d-attributes are exact mirror-image duplicates of the same 4 points, e.g. pause="M 117 267 L 117 297 L 62.5 297 L 62.5 327" vs resume="M 62.5 327 L 62.5 297 L 117 297 L 117 267"). The same root cause also affects the class fan case in the live view: opened the CLI's `vnm render fixtures/shop-class.mmd -f html` output and confirmed the 'has'/'visits'/'uses' edges from Dog all still start at the identical point (239.5, 290) — the exact shared-trunk pattern TEST-002 was about, minus the misleading diamond (which the interactive DOM never draws — a separate, pre-existing, documented tradeoff, not new). The drag-and-live-reroute MECHANISM itself is unaffected (confirmed: dragging 'Running' in the exported state.html recomputes all edge paths with zero console errors) — only the port-offset channel separation is missing from this code path.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE. Port the same offset mechanism into `src/render/dom/runtime.ts`: compute `computePortOffsets`-equivalent spreads for the live node positions (or thread the model's precomputed `edge.ports` through `buildPayload`/`serializeModel` into the runtime and have `routeEdgePath()`/`anchor()` accept and apply them, recomputing only the OFFSET, not which nodes/sides are grouped, since node identities don't change on drag) so `mount()`/`mountAsync()`/the custom element/the exported interactive HTML render the same channel-separated paths as the static SVG, both on initial mount and after a drag. Extend `test/dom-runtime-parity.test.ts` (which currently has no multi-edge-per-side case at all) with an anti-parallel pair AND a 3-edge fan, asserting the live runtime's edge paths for those cases equal `src/geometry`'s port-offset output — this is exactly the gap that let this regression through undetected.
issues.fix_summary: ROUND 6 (commit c6bec9a) — ported the whole port system into the interactive runtime. The .toString()-serialized vnmRuntime (src/render/dom/runtime.ts) now carries computePorts(), a twin of geometry.computePortOffsets (channel offsets + the TEST-006 label stagger) that recomputes from the LIVE positions on every render — so mount()/mountAsync()/the <very-nice-mermaid> element/the CLI's `-f html` export now render the SAME channel-separated paths as the static SVG, both on initial mount and after a drag (the live re-route no longer collapses edges back onto one channel). anchor() slides along the border by the offset (mirroring sidePoint/clampOffset); routeEdgePath() applies the channel offset to both endpoints + the label shift. VERIFIED hands-on on the CLI's own exported HTML: `vnm render fixtures/order-state.mmd -f html` — pause/resume now route on distinct 20u channels (M 97 267...52.5 vs M 72.5...117; previously exact mirror-duplicates at 117/62.5); start/stop leave/enter Idle's bottom at x=135.25 vs 155.25 (previously the same point). `vnm render fixtures/shop-class.mmd -f html` — has/visits/uses now start at 219.5/239.5/259.5 (previously all 239.5, the shared trunk). PARITY GUARD FIXED: test/dom-runtime-parity.test.ts previously computed its expectation WITHOUT ports (silently encoding the bug — its fan/converging edges spread in the static SVG but not the runtime, yet the test passed); it now threads e.ports and adds an anti-parallel pair, a 3-edge fan, AND a live-drag re-route (via importLayout) that all assert the runtime == computePortOffsets. NEW test/interactive-ports.test.ts EXECUTES the real exported HTML in jsdom (not the static SVG) and asserts (a) anti-parallel state transitions get distinct paths (Idle<->Running and Running<->Paused point-sets differ, no mirror-duplicate) and (b) the class has/visits/uses fan starts at 3 distinct points — closing the exact gap that hid this regression. Browser-safe core intact (runtime adds no imports; dist/index.js + dist/element.js still only lazy-import mermaid/jsdom/resvg). build+typecheck+test(266)+e2e(33) all green. ROUND 5 (commit 67b435c, static SVG only): computePortOffsets in src/geometry + src/layout spread anti-parallel pairs / fans onto distinct channels for the static SVG/PNG path; that never reached runtime.ts — the reopen.

issues.title: Fallback-tier CLI/jsdom degradation is far worse than the diagnostic wording ('geometry is approximate') suggests — 5 of 6 non-pie types render as a blank/invalid SVG, not merely imprecise
issues.description: For gantt/er/gitgraph/kanban/timeline (all tested via the built CLI to SVG and HTML), the emitted diagnostic is `render-degraded warn fallback capability=geometry '<type>' geometry is approximate under jsdom ... render in a browser for exact layout`. In practice the resulting SVG is not merely imprecise but effectively BLANK and, in gantt's case, spec-invalid: gantt's viewBox is `0 0 0 196` (zero width) and the rendered page is fully blank with 8 browser console errors (`<rect> attribute width: A negative value is not valid`, e.g. '-37.5', '-60'); er's viewBox is `-8 -8 28136 32` (28136px wide x 32px tall — collapsed to an invisible sliver); kanban's is `-10 -10 113212 36`; gitgraph and timeline show the same order-of-magnitude degenerate aspect ratio. Opening each in a real browser (screenshots fallback-gantt.png, fallback-er.png, fallback-kanban-jsdom.png) shows an entirely white page. This same degenerate SVG string is also baked directly into the `-f html` CLI export (confirmed: fb-gantt.html reproduces the identical 8 console errors and blank render when opened in a real browser) — i.e. the CLI's HTML output for these types is just as broken as the raw SVG, not re-rendered fresh in the browser. Only pie survives the jsdom path reasonably (correct pie geometry; its own degradation is milder — the legend text is clipped, e.g. 'Engine'/'Marke'/'Suppo', confirmed in fallback-pie.png/pie.png — because its layout doesn't depend on jsdom text measurement the way box/lane-based diagrams do). mindmap is the one type that correctly hard-fails (render-failed error, exit 1) rather than emitting a broken artifact. The 'warn'-level 'approximate' wording is a significant understatement for the 5 degenerate types and doesn't meet the spirit of FR5 ('transparent... so users won't need to debug this later') — a user trusting the diagnostic would expect a usable-but-imprecise SVG, not a blank one.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE. (1) Escalate the diagnostic severity/wording when the resulting geometry is genuinely degenerate (e.g. viewBox width or height below a small pixel threshold, or an aspect ratio beyond a sane bound, or the SVG contains negative width/height attributes) — emit `error` level or a distinctly-worded `render-degraded severe` code instead of reusing 'approximate', so --strict-less runs still surface how bad it is. (2) For `-f html`, either re-render fresh in the browser on load (matching D1's actual intent) instead of baking in the jsdom-time SVG, or clearly document/diagnose in the exported HTML itself that it needs a live browser re-render. (3) Consider hard-failing (mindmap's pattern) for types whose jsdom geometry is this consistently degenerate, rather than emitting a near-blank artifact with only a warn-level notice.
issues.fix_summary: D9-A honest failure. renderFallbackSvg (src/mermaid/fallback.ts) now detects a degenerate/blank headless render — zero/negative-area viewBox, extreme aspect (>8000px on an axis), a negative width/height rect, or empty content — and under jsdom emits a clear FR5 `fallback-render-unavailable` (error) diagnostic + throws FallbackUnavailableError instead of returning broken SVG. The CLI exits non-zero and writes NO svg/HTML file. A real browser (headless=false) still renders these types; pie (sane headless) is unaffected. CLI test: a degenerate ER render -> fallback-render-unavailable error, exit 1, and the output file is not written. (No Chromium path added — D9-B is separate.) ROUND 2 RE-TEST (2026-07-04): CLI-rendered a fresh gantt.mmd and er.mmd fixture to BOTH `-f svg` and `-f html`: both formats printed `vnm: fallback-render-unavailable error fallback '<type>' cannot be rendered headlessly (jsdom): the layout is degenerate/blank...`, exited 1, and wrote NO output file (confirmed via `ls` — file absent) for all 4 combinations. Confirmed pie still renders fine to both svg and html (exit 0, files written) and a native type (flowchart) is unaffected (exit 0). CONFIRMED FIXED.

issues.title: capability-unavailable (ascii, warn severity) exits non-zero by default for the fallback tier but exits 0 by default for the native tier — inconsistent, and contradicts FR5's own 'warn escalates only under --strict' design
issues.description: `vnm render <class-or-state> -f md` (no --strict) exits 0 with `capability-unavailable warn native capability=ascii ...` on stderr (confirmed for both class and state) — matches the existing CLI test's own description: 'graceful, non-zero only under --strict'. `vnm render <pie-or-gantt> -f md` (no --strict) exits 1 with the identical-severity `capability-unavailable warn fallback capability=ascii ...` diagnostic (confirmed for both pie and gantt) — this IS the currently-asserted behavior in test/cli.test.ts ('reports ASCII/Markdown as unavailable for a fallback type (FR4)' asserts `r.status` toBe(1) with no --strict flag), so this is an intentional asymmetry, not an accidental regression, but it is inconsistent: the same severity (`warn`) diagnostic for the same capability gap (ascii unavailable) behaves differently depending only on which tier produced it. This contradicts FR5's stated escalation model ('--strict escalates degradations to a non-zero exit; never silently fall back' implies warn-level notices are non-fatal by default, only escalated under --strict) and the native tier's own precedent.
issues.proposed_solution: NEEDS-USER-DECISION. Recommend aligning the fallback-tier ascii-unavailable path in cli/run.ts with the native-tier one (graceful exit 0 by default, exit 1 only under --strict), updating the existing test/cli.test.ts expectation ('reports ASCII/Markdown as unavailable for a fallback type (FR4)') to assert status 0 by default and 1 under --strict, matching the class/state precedent. Flagging as a decision rather than a silent fix because it requires changing an existing, currently-passing, intentionally-written test assertion — worth confirming the desired default before an agent flips it.
issues.fix_summary: D8-A. The fallback-tier ascii capability-unavailable path in cli/run.ts now returns `strict ? 1 : 0` (was an unconditional 1), matching the native tier: a warn-level capability loss is graceful (exit 0) by default and non-zero only under --strict. The previously-asserted test/cli.test.ts expectation was updated; new CLI tests assert BOTH tiers exit 0 by default and 1 under --strict, and that native + fallback ascii-unavailable exit codes are consistent. ROUND 2 RE-TEST (2026-07-04): confirmed via the CLI directly — `class -f md` and `state -f md` (native) exit 0 by default / 1 under --strict; `pie -f md` (fallback) exit 0 by default / 1 under --strict; all four print the same `capability-unavailable warn ... capability=ascii` diagnostic shape. CONFIRMED FIXED and consistent.

issues.title: Static SVG anti-parallel fix separates the edge PATHS onto distinct channels, but the two labels' background plates are close enough to overlap — one label's text is visually clipped by the other's plate, leaving it unreadable (e.g. 'start'->'st', 'pause'->'p', 'go'->~90% hidden)
issues.description: New finding, round 2 (2026-07-04), surfaced while re-verifying TEST-003's fix. `computePortOffsets` (src/geometry/index.ts) spreads anti-parallel edge anchors onto two channels a fixed `PORT_STEP` (20 SVG units, clamped further by border length) apart — this genuinely separates the LINE geometry (confirmed: both arrowheads visible, distinct paths). But each edge's label is still drawn as a background `<rect>` (sized to the label text width, typically 27-70+ units for real words) centered on its own channel's midpoint, independent of the OTHER edge's plate — when two channels are only ~20 units apart and either label is more than ~20 units wide (essentially any real word), the two plates overlap, and since SVG paints in document order, the later-drawn plate's opaque background clips part of the earlier label's text underneath it. Confirmed via raw SVG source on the CLI's own output (not just a screenshot artifact): fixtures/order-state.mmd -> SVG — 'start' rect spans x=82.43-135.83, 'stop' rect spans x=106.77-151.49 (drawn after, so it covers the right ~2/3 of 'start'); rendered/screenshotted result: 'start' reads as 'st', 'stop' reads correctly. Same for 'pause' (rect 36.05-89.45) vs 'resume' (rect 51.71-113.79, drawn after): 'pause' reads as 'p'. A flowchart repro (`flowchart TD\nA -->|go| B\nB -->|back| A`) shows the same pattern even more severely: 'go' rect x=12.32-39.68 vs 'back' rect x=23.64-68.36 (drawn after) — screenshot test/screenshots/round2-flowchart-antiparallel-labels-overlap.png shows 'go' reduced to an unreadable sliver of its first letter, 'back' fully legible. The class fan case (TEST-002) is NOT affected — dagre naturally spreads a fan's distinct target nodes far enough apart that their labels don't collide (confirmed: has/visits/uses label rects in fixtures/shop-class.mmd's SVG have zero x-overlap). This is a real, screenshot- and source-confirmed legibility regression exposed by (not present before) the TEST-002/003 channel-separation fix: for a labeled anti-parallel pair, a user can now see there ARE two distinct transitions/edges (a genuine improvement over full occlusion) but often cannot read one of the two labels' text.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE. When placing an anti-parallel pair's two label plates, either (a) offset the label positions further apart than the line channels (e.g. stagger them vertically/along-path as well as by channel, not just reuse the channel's own midpoint), or (b) detect when two channels' label plates would overlap (compare rect bounds before drawing) and increase the channel spacing (or stagger the along-path midpoint fraction, e.g. 40%/60% instead of both at 50%) until they clear, or (c) raise `PORT_STEP` when both edges in a group carry a label. Add a regression test rendering an anti-parallel pair with two real-word labels of typical length and asserting their computed label-plate rects do not intersect.
issues.fix_summary: Fixed in the shared geometry (commit 77fbde2) so BOTH the static SVG and the runtime stagger colliding labels. computePortOffsets (src/geometry/index.ts) now takes each labelled edge's plate size (w,h — the same maxChars*fontSize*0.62+10 / lines*lineHeight+4 formula the SVG sink + runtime use) and, for edges that share the SAME unordered node pair (an anti-parallel or parallel bundle — a FAN of edges to different targets does NOT group, so the class case stays untouched), packs their plates end-to-end along the pair's dominant axis (extent along that axis + PORT_LABEL_PAD=6 between adjacent plates) and centres them on the shared midpoint. The per-edge shift rides on RoutedEdge.ports.labelShift so applyPositions + the state pseudo-state shrink reproduce it; routeEdge adds it to the label position. The DOM runtime's computePorts() twin recomputes the identical stagger from live positions (so a drag keeps labels legible). Verified: for the flowchart repro (A<->B, go/back) the two plates now clear by 6u in TD (staggered in y) and in LR (staggered in x); for order-state, start/stop and pause/resume plates no longer intersect (`start` reads fully, not `st`). Only the state SVG snapshots changed (labels moved; paths identical) — the class fan snapshot is unchanged (fans don't group). Regression tests: TD+LR bidirectional labelled pairs (test/layout.test.ts) + state start/stop (test/state-svg.test.ts) assert the computed plate rects don't intersect + that a labelShift actually fired. build+typecheck+test(266)+e2e(33) green.
